entry.title: SAP Sustainability Control Tower: ESRS S1-6 – Characteristics of the undertaking’s employees
entry.content:
Disclaimer: This blog post reflects the state of the SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) as of February 11th, 2025.

ESRS S1-6 requires a company to disclose quantitative and qualitative (/narrative) information about its employees. SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) provides out of the box several quantitative metrics, hence this article focusses on how to bridge the gap for the ESRS S1-6 requirements for the quantitative data points. Specifically, the focus is on paragraph 50 a: "the undertaking shall disclose the total number of employees by head count, and breakdowns by gender and by country for countries in which the undertaking has 50 or more employees representing at least 10% of its total number of employees." 

Total Number of Employees

For the “total number of employees by head count” the SAP provided metric Total Number of Employees can be used.  

Breakdown by Gender

The breakdown by gender is currently not out of the box possible in SCT. In the additional application requirement 55 (AR 55) it is described that the company shall disclose the information in a tabular format and provides the following template:

As of now SCT doesn’t allow to display tables. However, it is possible to visualize the breakdown by gender with Standard Custom Metrics. You can create a Standard Custom Metric within the Manage Metrics app:

Then you can define the details of the Standard Custom Metric. In this case, for Z_1-16_FEMALE:

Please ensure that your leading and supported reporting hierarchies are set correctly.

I.e., if you provide information via the Measure “Workforce Headcount” and its template “Workforce Headcount – Actuals” in the Manage ESG Data app, you need to ensure that the respective hierarchies are maintained if you want them to be supported in SCT.

  • ID_COMPANY_CODE stands for Legal Entity
  • ID_ORG_UNIT stands for Reporting Line
  • ID_BUSINESS_LOC stands for Business Location

In the example above all hierarchies are provided with data and hence should be supported.

In the calculation definition you can select the Measure “Workforce Headcount”, the Calculation Method is Sum, and you select the respective filter to sum up. In this case you sum up all entries with Gender Female.  As the requirement is to disclose the headcount it is crucial to set the Value Type to Row Count.

Background: In your template you define both, MEASURE_VALUE_CHAR and MEASURE_COUNT. For the “Workforce Headcount” the former defines the full time equivalent (FTE) of each entry whereas the latter defines the headcount. In this simplified example there are in total 275 FTEs and the headcount is 350. This can be the case due to part time working employees. When defining the calculation in SCT the value type defines whether is should sum up the aggregated values (in this case the MEASURE_VALUE_CHAR – FTE) or the rows (in this case MEASURE_COUNT – headcount).

After creating the Standard Custom Metric and uploading the “Workforce Headcount – Actuals” template in the Manage ESG Data app, you can then calculate the data for the Standard Custom Metrics, and publish it. E.g., for Z_S1-6_FEMALE:

After the calculation you can already see that SCT selected the correct entries to be summed up:

In the Analyze ESG Data app you can the visualize all calculated Standard Metrics and SAP provided Metrics:

Breakdown by country for countries in which the undertaking has 50 or more employees representing at least 10% of its total number of employees.

The breakdown by country with the respective filtering of 50+ employees and representing at least 10% of the company’s total number of employees is currently not out of the box possible in SCT. In the additional application requirement 55 (AR 55) it is described that the company shall disclose the information in a tabular format and provides the following template:

As of now SCT doesn’t allow to display tables. However, it is possible to visualize the breakdown by countries with the Our Ambitions app. If you drill down the Total Number of Employees Metric under the Business Location hierarchy, you can visualize the breakdown per country. Please note that this requires the appropriate set-up of the master data and the provided actuals. Furthermore, a filtering of 50+ employees and representing at least 10% of the company’s total number of employees is also here not possible.

entry.title: Re: Item category L in RFQ doc category R
entry.content:
Hi,

Can you please check the SPRO>MM>Purchasing>RFQ and Quotation> Define Doc types for RFQ, and confirm if you Link the PR document type here. Also double check the Allowed item Categories for RFQ doc type.

Share screenshot if you have any questions.

Thanks,

Sanan

entry.title: SAP BTP: “Change Agent” or “Scale Agent” Part 7 – How to select Data & Analytics BTP Services
entry.content:
SAP Business Technology Platform offers a robust suite of over 80 services, many of which play critical roles in supporting enterprise-level operations. The challenge lies in determining the right combination of services tailored to specific use cases. Crafting an effective SAP BTP solution requires a deep understanding of the platform. While established architectural patterns provide a solid foundation, assessing individual use cases to identify the most suitable architecture is a skill that requires experience and strategic evaluation.

 

How to decide when to use what services in SAP BTP

 

To effectively choose the right services, it is essential to understand the core archetypes within SAP BTP, which include:

  • Application Modernization
  • Data Modernization
  • AI & Generative AI

 

The first step in determining the appropriate services is to classify your application as either transactional or analytical. Transactional applications typically align with the Application Modernization archetype, while analytical applications are categorized under Data Modernization. This classification serves as the foundation for building an application on SAP BTP.

 

Once the core archetype is defined, further enhancements can be incorporated, particularly with AI or Generative AI services, which fall under the AI & Gen AI archetype. For a broader understanding of the classification, please refer to Figure 1.

 

 

Figure 1 – Determining application as Transactional or Analytical

 

After identifying the correct use-case type, you can begin designing the appropriate architecture. This step is crucial for determining which SAP BTP services are essential for implementing and optimizing the solution.

 

Data Modernization archetype

 

Applications categorized as analytical fall under the Data Modernization archetype, highlighting their role in transforming and enhancing data processes and infrastructure. This categorization is essential as it aids organizations in identifying and leveraging technological advancements to optimize data management and utilization.

 

Figure 3 below provides a decision flow to further illustrate this classification and guide stakeholders in understanding and applying these principles effectively. By adhering to these frameworks, businesses can enhance their data processing capabilities, ensuring more efficient and insightful data-driven decision-making.

 

 

Figure 3 – Data Modernization archetype decision matrix

 

Step-1 – Business Intelligence

 

Ask if it's a standard report or dashboard accessed from a single source; if yes, use SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

Creating non-standard custom reports in SAP S/4HANA and extending them with SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) can greatly enhance your reporting capabilities. Here are some examples where custom reports can be developed:

 

Application Type

Finance

Sales, Service & Marketing

Procurement & Expense Management

Supply Chain

Human Resources

Operations

IT

Multi source Financial Analysis Report

X

 

 

 

 

 

 

Procurement and Supplier Performance Dashboard

 

 

X

 

 

 

 

Sales Pipeline & Revenue Forecast Report

X

X

 

 

 

 

 

Manufacturing Efficiency & Downtime Analysis

Customer Profitability and Segmentation Report

X

Environment, Social & Governance (ESG) Report

X

 

X

X

X

X

X

Consolidated Budget vs Actuals Report

X

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  • **Multi-Source Financial Analysis Report**:

   - **Objective**: Aggregate financial data from different source systems or modules like FI, CO, and external databases.

   - **Features**: Real-time currency conversion, dynamic profit center hierarchy adjustments, and custom KPIs.

   - **SAC Integration**: Use SAC’s Visualization tools for dynamic dashboards and predictive analytics to forecast financial trends.

  • **Procurement and Supplier Performance Dashboard**:

   - **Objective**: Evaluate and monitor supplier performance in real-time, integrating data from procurement, quality management, and external vendor ratings.

   - **Features**: Supplier scorecards, lead time analysis, and quality non-conformance rates.

   - **SAC Integration**: Create interactive dashboards with scorecards and trend analyses to manage supplier risks and opportunities.

 

  • **Sales Pipeline and Revenue Forecast Report**:

   - **Objective**: Provide insights into sales pipeline status and revenue projections.

   - **Features**: Sales cycle analysis, probability-adjusted forecast, and win/loss ratios.

   - **SAC Integration**: Use predictive analytics to forecast sales and identify the highest impact leads using machine learning models.

 

  • **Manufacturing Efficiency and Downtime Analysis**:

   - **Objective**: Track production efficiency and downtime to improve operational performance.

   - **Features**: Machine-level efficiency reports, downtime causes analysis, and OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) calculation.

   - **SAC Integration**: Use SAC to visualize production KPIs and create automated alerts for operator action.

 

  • **Customer Profitability and Segmentation Report**:

   - **Objective**: Analyze customer data to understand profitability and identify high-value segments.

   - **Features**: Profitability scorecards, churn analysis, and segment-based profit comparisons.

   - **SAC Integration**: Utilize SAC for customer profitability analysis with segment drill-downs and advanced data visualizations.

 

  • **Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) Report**:

   - **Objective**: Compile and analyze ESG metrics for sustainability reporting.

   - **Features**: Carbon footprint analysis, diversity metrics, and compliance tracking.

   - **SAC Integration**: Leverage SAC to create comprehensive ESG dashboards with benchmarks and performance indicators.

 

  • **Consolidated Budget vs. Actuals Report**:

   - **Objective**: Compare budgeted figures against actuals across various organizational units.

   - **Features**: Variance analysis, periodic trends, and multi-level budget hierarchy reports.

   - **SAC Integration**: Use SAC’s planning capabilities to simulate future budget scenarios and variances.

 

To create these custom reports, collaboration between business analysts, report developers, and subject matter experts is essential. SAC’s capabilities in data connectivity, visualization, and machine learning provide a robust platform to build and deploy these tools effectively. Always ensure data integrity and security when handling sensitive business information across these platforms.

 

Step-2 – Business Data Fabric

Consider if data from multiple sources is needed; if yes, employ SAP Datasphere for a unified enterprise data view.

Cocreating a data fabric infused with context and governance for true business-wide data-driven decision-making

SAP Datasphere, formerly known as SAP Data Warehouse Cloud, is a comprehensive data management solution designed to integrate, manage, and analyze data across an enterprise. With its capabilities, businesses can build various kinds of applications and solutions, including:

 

  • **Data Warehousing Applications**:

   - Centralize data from disparate sources for more accessible reporting and analytics.

   - Create a unified view of organizational data to streamline business intelligence processes.

 

  1. **Business Analytics Applications**:

   - Develop robust analytical dashboards and reports using data models and visualization tools.

   - Leverage prebuilt connectors for SAP and non-SAP sources for comprehensive insights.

 

  • **Data Integration Solutions**:

   - Integrate and harmonize data from various sources, such as on-premise databases, cloud services, and external systems.

   - Establish real-time data pipelines to ensure up-to-date insights.

 

  • **Financial and Operational Planning**:

   - Implement planning solutions that integrate financial, sales, and operational data for comprehensive financial forecasting and analysis.

   - Facilitate scenario planning and what-if analysis.

 

  • **Predictive and Advanced Analytics**:

   - Build predictive models using integrated Machine Learning services to forecast trends and behaviors.

   - Use advanced analytics to derive deeper insights from structured and unstructured data.

 

  • **Contextualized Data Access**:

   - Provide tailored data access to various business units, ensuring relevant insights are easily accessible.

   - Enhance decision-making by delivering contextualized data in real-time.

 

  • **Data-Driven Applications**:

   - Develop applications that consume and display data insights directly to end-users, integrating analytics into business processes.

   - Create SaaS offerings for clients that leverage their data for enhanced productivity and operations.

 

  • **IoT and Big Data Analytics**:

   - Harness IoT data by integrating with SAP’s IoT solutions and processing large volumes of data efficiently.

   - Analyze big data sets to extract actionable insights for operational improvements.

 

  • **Customer and Supply Chain Insights**:

   - Develop customer analytics solutions to improve customer experience through insights on behavior and preferences.

   - Optimize supply chain operations with data insights into logistics, inventory, and supplier performance.

 

The flexibility and scalability of SAP Datasphere allow businesses to innovate and quickly adapt to changing data landscapes, thereby fostering data-driven decision-making across the organization.

 

Step-3 – Predictive Analytics

Determine if predictive capabilities are necessary; if yes, add HANA Cloud to your data platform and business intelligence.

 

SAP HANA Cloud's predictive capabilities can be applied across various lines of business (LOB) to provide valuable insights and enhance decision-making processes. Here are some common use cases by LOB:

### Finance

  1. **Cash Flow Forecasting**: Predict cash flow to optimize liquidity and financial planning.
  2. **Fraud Detection**: Identify unusual patterns that may indicate fraudulent activities.
  3. **Credit Risk Assessment**: Evaluate the creditworthiness of customers and predict potential defaults.
  4. **Expense Management**: Analyze expense patterns to forecast future costs and optimize budgets.

 

### Marketing

  1. **Customer Segmentation**: Identify distinct customer groups based on purchasing behaviors and preferences.
  2. **Churn Prediction**: Predict which customers are likely to leave and design retention strategies.
  3. **Campaign Effectiveness**: Analyze past campaign performances to predict the success of future initiatives.
  4. **Market Basket Analysis**: Understand product associations and optimize cross-selling and up-selling strategies.

 

### Sales

  1. **Demand Forecasting**: Predict future sales volumes to optimize inventory and supply chain operations.
  2. **Lead Scoring**: Rank leads based on their likelihood to convert to closed deals.
  3. **Revenue Forecasting**: Estimate future revenue based on historical data and market trends.
  4. **Sales Pipeline Analysis**: Predict the closure of deals and optimize sales strategies.

 

### Human Resources

  1. **Employee Turnover Prediction**: Forecast employee attrition and identify factors contributing to turnover.
  2. **Talent Acquisition**: Predict the success of job candidates based on past hiring data.
  3. **Skill Gap Analysis**: Identify gaps in employee skills and predict future training needs.
  4. **Workforce Planning**: Model future workforce requirements based on business growth scenarios.

 

### Supply Chain

  1. **Inventory Optimization**: Predict inventory levels to reduce carrying costs and prevent stockouts.
  2. **Demand Planning**: Forecast demand fluctuations and align supply chain operations accordingly.
  3. **Supplier Risk Assessment**: Evaluate risks associated with suppliers based on historical performance.
  4. **Logistics Optimization**: Predict logistics bottlenecks and optimize transportation routes.

 

### Production

  1. **Predictive Maintenance**: Forecast machinery breakdowns and plan maintenance activities proactively.
  2. **Quality Control**: Predict defects and improve quality control processes.
  3. **Capacity Planning**: Forecast production needs to optimize capacity utilization.
  4. **Waste Reduction**: Identify patterns contributing to waste and optimize processes for sustainability.

 

### IT and Operations

  1. **System Outage Prediction**: Predict and prevent IT system failures and downtimes.
  2. **Resource Optimization**: Forecast IT resource needs to optimize infrastructure and reduce costs.
  3. **Security Threat Analysis**: Predict and prevent potential cybersecurity threats.
  4. **Process Automation**: Use predictive insights to enhance

 

Step-4 – Planning

Evaluate if planning capabilities are required; if yes, include SAP Analytics Cloud planning solutions with your data platform and business intelligence.

 

SAP Analytics Cloud Planning is a versatile tool that can be applied across various lines of business (LOBs) to facilitate better decision-making through integrated planning, analysis, and insights. Here are some common use cases for different lines of business:

  • **Finance:**

   - Financial Planning and Analysis (FP&A)

   - Budgeting and Forecasting

   - Profitability and Cost Management

   - Cash Flow Management

   - Consolidation and Financial Close

   - Scenario Planning

 

  • **Sales:**

   - Sales Forecasting and Planning

   - Territory and Quota Management

   - Revenue Planning

   - Sales Performance Analysis

   - Customer and Product Profitability Analysis

 

  • **Human Resources:**

   - Workforce Planning and Analytics

   - Headcount and Capacity Planning

   - Compensation Planning and Analysis

   - Talent Management and Retention

   - Diversity and Inclusion Analytics

 

  • **Operations:**

   - Demand and Supply Planning

   - Inventory and Production Planning

   - Resource Optimization

   - Logistics and Distribution Planning

   - Quality Management and Monitoring

 

  • **Marketing:**

   - Marketing Spend and ROI Analysis

   - Campaign Planning and Effectiveness

   - Customer Segmentation and Profiling

   - Brand Performance and Competitive Analysis

   - Product Launch Planning

 

  • **IT:**

   - IT Budgeting and Cost Management

   - Project Portfolio Management

   - Infrastructure and Resource Planning

   - IT Service Performance Analysis

   - Cybersecurity and Risk Management

 

  • **Procurement:**

   - Spend Analysis and Procurement Planning

   - Supplier Performance and Risk Management

   - Contract Management and Compliance

   - Sourcing and Category Management

   - Cost Reduction Strategies

 

  • **Supply Chain:**

   - End-to-End Supply Chain Visibility

   - Demand Planning and Forecasting

   - Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP)

   - Supplier Collaboration and Risk Management

   - Inventory Optimization

 

These use cases highlight the flexibility of SAP Analytics Cloud Planning in addressing the specific needs and objectives of various departments within an organization, enabling integrated planning and informed decision-making across the enterprise.

 

Step-5 – Custom Reporting or Analytics

For needs not covered above, consider a mix of SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Datasphere, SAP Data Intelligence, and other SAP BTP services.

 

SAP Analytics Cloud and Planning can be applied to a variety of complex and nuanced use cases across different lines of business (LOBs). While there are many common applications, here are some of the more intricate and less common use cases that leverage the full capability of SAP Analytics Cloud along with specific solutions:

 

Application Type

Finance

Sales, Service & Marketing

Procurement & Expense Management

Supply Chain

Human Resources

Operations

IT

Demand Sensing and Shaping

 

 

 

X

 

 

 

Integrated Business Planning

 

 

 

X

 

 

 

Talent Retention & Development Strategy

 

 

 

 

X

 

 

Scenario based Risk-Management

X

 

 

 

 

 

 

Strategic Workforce Planning linked with Financials

X

 

 

 

X

 

 

R&D Investment Valuation

 

 

 

 

 

X

 

Lifestyle analytics for Product Innovation

 

 

 

 

 

X

 

Predictive Maintenance with IOT Data

 

 

 

 

 

X

 

Production Yield Optimization

 

 

 

 

 

X

 

Carbon Footprint and Environmental Impact Analysis

X

Omni-channel Strategy Optimization:

Personalized Promotions and Pricing Strategies

Infrastructure Lifecycle Management

 

 

 

 

 

X

 

Urban Mobility & Transportation Freecasting

X

 

X

 

 

 

 

 

 

  • **Advanced Supply Chain Planning:**

   - **Demand Sensing and Shaping:** Use predictive analytics to enhance demand forecasting with real-time data inputs like social media trends, weather patterns, and economic indicators.

   - **Integrated Business Planning (IBP):** Complex scenarios where finance, sales, and operations planning are integrated to optimize inventory levels and production schedules.

 

  • **Human Capital Analytics:**

   - **Talent Retention and Development Strategy:** By analyzing employee performance, engagement surveys, and external benchmark data, the tool can help predict turnover risk and formulate talent development strategies.

   - **Diversity and Inclusion Impact Analysis:** Assess the effectiveness of diversity initiatives on organizational performance through scenario modeling.

 

  • **Complex Financial Planning and Analysis:**

   - **Scenario-based Risk Management:** Running multiple stress-test scenarios to assess financial risks under various economic conditions with real-time data adjustments.

   - **Strategic Workforce Planning linked with Financials:** Integration of workforce cost planning with long-term financial forecasts to assess the strategic implications of workforce decisions.

 

  • **High-Tech and R&D Portfolio Optimization:**

   - **R&D Investment Valuation:** Simulate and forecast the potential return on investment for various R&D projects using historical data and predictive analytics.

   - **Lifecycle Analytics for Product Innovation:** Analyze the entire lifecycle from ideation to market penetration and end-of-life, optimizing resources and timing for product release.

 

  • **Complex Manufacturing Process Analytics:**

   - **Predictive Maintenance with IoT Data:** Utilizing IoT sensor data to predict equipment failures and optimize maintenance schedules.

   - **Production Yield Optimization:** Analyzing the various parameters that impact production quality and yield, and implementing scenario planning to increase efficiency.

 

  • **Cross-Industry Sustainability and Regulatory Reporting:**

   - **Carbon Footprint and Environmental Impact Analysis:** Complex modeling involving environmental data to forecast carbon footprint and align with sustainability targets.

   - **Compliance and Risk Management Reporting:** Dynamic modeling of regulatory changes and their impact on business operations and financial performance.

 

  • **Retail and Consumer Behavior Analytics:**

   - **Omni-channel Strategy Optimization:** Evaluating customer touchpoints and optimizing marketing spend across various channels to maximize ROI.

   - **Personalized Promotions and Pricing Strategies:** Advanced analytics to tailor promotions at an individual customer level based on purchase history and predictive behavior analysis.

 

  • **Smart Infrastructure and Urban Planning:**

   - **   :** Planning and analyzing the long-term sustainability and cost implications of infrastructure projects.

   - **Urban Mobility and Transportation Forecasting:** Visualizing and forecasting urban travel patterns to improve city planning and public transport efficiency.

 

These complex use cases require a deep integration of multiple data sources, sophisticated analytical models, and cross-functional collaboration, often involving advanced features of SAP Analytics Cloud like predictive analytics, machine learning, and integration with other SAP and non-SAP systems.

 

 

Conclusion

 

In conclusion, designing an optimal SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) solution requires a thorough understanding of the platform’s diverse services and their application to specific business use-cases. By carefully evaluating whether an application is transactional or analytical, businesses can select the appropriate archetype—such as Application Modernization, Data Modernization, or AI & Generative AI—ensuring that the right SAP BTP services are deployed to meet their needs.

 

The platform offers a range of services, from SAP Analytics Cloud for reporting and planning to SAP Datasphere for data foundation and SAP HANA Cloud for predictive analytics and vector engine. By leveraging these tools, organizations can enhance their data processing capabilities, improve decision-making with advanced analytics, and foster innovation across their operations. Customization of reporting, data integration, and predictive capabilities enables businesses to drive more efficient, insightful, and forward-thinking outcomes, establishing a strong foundation for both present needs and future growth.

 

Thanks for your time in reading this blog, will see you in the next blog in this series SAP BTP from “Change Agent” to “Scale Agent”.

entry.title: Content Modifier Pallet in SAP CPI
entry.content:
Content Modifier: A content modifier is a tool used in integration processes to modify the content of a message as it passes   through the system. It allows you to add, change, or remove parts of the message, such as headers, properties, and the message body itself. This is particularly useful in scenarios where you need to adapt    the message format or content to meet the requirements of different systems or services involved in the integration.

In a content modifier, we have three parts: the Exchange Property, the Message Header, and the Message Body.

Exchange Property: These are temporary and exist only for the lifetime of the message transaction. They are used within the iFlow.

Message Header: Headers travel along with the message to the receiver and are used globally.

Message Body: In the message body, we add Apache Camel expressions or files. These files can be either XML or CSV.

 

Here in exchange property, we are using source type is XPath.The Name PID is picks the PersonalID details from the source value (here we give Xpath of the xml). when we use Xpath in source type it picks only one field from xml which we define in property or header.

 

In the second Content Modifier, the message body part we are using expressions to call header&property from the xml which are define in first content modifier. To call header: ${header.<HeaderName>}

To call property: ${property.<propertyName>}

 

 

And the Output is here:

entry.title: Elevate Your SAC Dashboards: Unleash the Power of Custom R Visualizations!
entry.content:
Elevate Your SAC Dashboards: Unleash the Power of Custom R Visualizations!

In the world of business intelligence and analytics, visual storytelling is a powerful tool for uncovering insights and driving informed decision-making. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), with its robust dashboarding and analytics capabilities, stands as a leader in this domain. But what if you need to go beyond its native visualizations? Enter R, the widely used programming language for statistical computing and graphics.

By combining the flexibility of R's visualization libraries with SAC's interactive dashboards, you can create custom widgets that elevate your analytics game. From dynamic heatmaps to intricate network diagrams, R empowers you to craft visuals that cater to unique business requirements, enabling advanced storytelling and better decision support through widgets, such as Gauge Charts, in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC). We’ll guide you through the setup process, demonstrate a practical example using ggplot2, and share best practices for seamlessly integrating R-powered widgets into SAC dashboards. Whether you’re a data scientist, business analyst, or SAC user looking to push the boundaries of analytics, this guide will unlock new opportunities to enhance your SAC dashboards with custom, dynamic visualizations.

 Let’s dive in and transform your analytics experience!

Set Up R Integration in SAC

Before you can start creating R-powered charts, you need to ensure that SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is properly connected to your R environment. This integration is essential for running R scripts and embedding custom visualizations like Gauge Charts into your SAC dashboards.

  1. Access SAC:
  • Log in to your SAP Analytics Cloud account: Ensure you have the necessary access rights to create stories, dashboards, and use custom widgets in SAC.
  1. Activate R Script Integration:
  • Go to the SAC Administration Page:
    • Navigate to System > Administration > Connectivity.

 

 

    • Here, check and ensure R integration is available and enabled.
  • Ensure the R Environment is Ready:
    • SAC requires an external R server or R environment to execute the scripts.
    • Make sure that R packages like ggplot2, plotly, or any other visualization libraries are installed on your R environment, as these will be used to create custom charts.
  1. Verify Configuration:
  • Check Connection Details: Ensure that SAC is correctly configured to connect with the R server.
  • If possible, run a test script from SAC to confirm that the R integration is working properly.

Here’s a step-by-step guide to creating and using a custom R-Widget in your SAC Story.

Step 1: Add a Custom Widget:

  • Click on + Add or Insert in the Story workspace.
  • Select Others => R Visualization.

 

 

Step 2: On the Builder pane,

  • Choose + Add Input Data, where you can select the model from where the data needs to be used for the widget
  • You have the flexibility to choose Input from more than one model

 

 

  • Double click on the model name and Select the Measures and Dimensions to be used in the widget from the Builder.
  • Using the preview option(Expand the window by clicking the below icon on the top right)

 

 

Step 3: On the Builder pane,

  • Choose + Edit Script, where we will write the R script for Gauge meter/Taco meter
  • The Script Tab for an R Widget is divided into four sections, each serving a specific purpose to configure and execute the widget. Here's an overview of these sections
  1. Editor is where you write and manage your R script.
  2. The Environment section displays the objects (e.g., dataframes, variables) created during the script execution.
  3. Console shows real-time outputs, logs, and error messages generated by your R script.
  4. The Preview section renders the output of your R script, such as charts or visualizations.

 

  • On the Editor space use the below R-Script for Gauge meter,
  •  

library(plotly)

library(ggplot2)

# Create the Gauge Chart using Plotly

fig <- plot_ly(

  domain = list(x = c(0, 10000), y = c(0, 10000)),  # Define the size of the plot

  value = Model1$Count,  # Current value from the data model (replace Model1$Count with actual value)

  title = list(text = "Sales Performance", font = list(size = 10)),  # Title of the gauge

  type = "indicator",  # Define the chart type as "indicator" for the gauge

  mode = "gauge+number",  # Display both the gauge and the number

  number = list(font = list(color = "black", size = 20)),  # Number formatting in the center of the gauge

  gauge = list(

    # Set the gauge axis with range limits and tick values

    axis = list(

      range = c(0, MD_XXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total),  # Define the range for the gauge axis

      tickwidth = 1,  # Set the width of ticks

      tickcolor = "darkblue",  # Set the tick color

      tickmode = "array",  # Use array for ticks

      tickvals = c(0, MD_XXXXX$`Sales Target Ct-YTD`, MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total)  # Define the tick values

    ),

    bar = list(color = "black"),  # Set the color of the gauge bar

    steps = list(

      # Define color-coded steps for different performance ranges

      list(range = c(0, (MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total * 30 / 100)), color = "Red"),  # Red step (below 30% target)

      list(range = c((MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total * 30 / 100), (MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total * 65 / 100)), color = "Yellow"),  # Yellow step (30-65% target)

      list(range = c((MD_XXXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total * 65 / 100), MD_XXXXX$Sales_Target_Count_Total), color = "Green")  # Green step (above 65% target)

    ),

    threshold = list(

      line = list(color = "Limegreen", width = 4),  # Define the threshold line with color and width

      thickness = 0.75,  # Set the thickness of the threshold line

      value = MD_XXXXX$`Sales Target Ct-YTD`  # The threshold value (YTD sales target)

    )

  )

)

 

# Adjust layout (margins, etc.)

fig <- fig %>%

  layout(

    margin = list(l = 20, r = 50, t = 10, b = 10) 

  )

# Display the gauge chart

Fig

 

 

Step 3: After adding the script, click Run or Execute to run the R script.

  • The Gauge Meter should now be displayed with the values and colors dynamically set based on the data in the input model.

Value: The gauge will display the current value (e.g., Model1$Count) in the center of the chart.

Thresholds and Colors: Based on the Sales Target, the gauge will show color-coded sections:

  • Red for values below 30% of the target.
  • Yellow for values between 30% and 65% of the target.
  • Green for values above 65% of the target.

 Dynamic Updates: The gauge will dynamically update based on the values from the data model, ensuring that your visualizations stay accurate and up-to-date.

 

 

  •  If needed, adjust the layout, margins, and colors to match your desired visual theme.

One of the powerful features of SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is its ability to dynamically link custom R widgets like your Gauge Meter to page or story filters, as well as perform linked analysis from other tables or charts. This makes the widget even more interactive and responsive to user input.

By the end of this blog, you will have a clear understanding of how to integrate R-powered visualizations like Gauge meter into your SAC dashboards, and how to leverage the interactive features like filters and linked analysis. This will enable you to create more dynamic, user-driven dashboards that provide deeper insights and a more engaging analytics experience.

entry.title: SAP IBP Configuration - Create a Alert Key Figure in SAP IBP
entry.content:
An alert key figure in SAP IBP is a specific type of key figure used to monitor and trigger alerts based on predefined conditions or thresholds. These key figures help identify issues or exceptions in the supply chain, such as capacity overloads, stock shortages, or demand fluctuations. Here's a bit more detail:

  1. Definition:
    • Alert key figures are quantitative or qualitative values that are monitored to detect deviations from expected performance.
    • They are configured in the backend system, where you set the conditions and thresholds that trigger the alerts.
  2. Usage:
    • These key figures are used in various planning processes, such as sales and operations planning (S&OP), demand planning, and supply planning.
    • When the conditions defined for an alert key figure are met, an alert is generated, which can be viewed in the SAP IBP Excel add-in or the relevant SAP Fiori apps.
  3. Configuration:
    • To create or modify an alert key figure, you typically use the Planning Area app in SAP IBP. Here, you can define the logic and thresholds for the alerts.
    • Alerts can be customized to suit specific business needs, such as monitoring weekly split ratios or capacity utilization.

These are key figures with user-defined criteria that monitor and manage execution of business plans. They can only have values of 0 or 1, meaning that the alert itself is either ON or OFF. Alerts typically check conditions on other key figures, such as TargetRev versus ConsensusRev ＞10%.

In this article, we will define an alert key figure, targetrevalert, with condition,  TARGETREVENUE > CONSENSUSDEMANDREV.

Create alert key figure, targetrevalert

1.  In planning area, create alert key figure.

Add the calculation: 

TARGETREVALERT@PERPRODCUSTCURR = IF("TARGETREVENUE@PERPRODCUSTCURR" > "CONSENSUSDEMANDREVCURR@PERPRODCUSTCURR",1,0)

The formula is designed to create an alert based on a comparison between target revenue and consensus demand revenue for a specific product, customer, and currency. Here's a breakdown of what it does:

  • TARGETREVALERT@PERPRODCUSTCURR: This is the key figure that will store the alert value.
  • IF: This function checks a condition and returns one value if the condition is true and another if it is false.
  • "TARGETREVENUE@PERPRODCUSTCURR" > "CONSENSUSDEMANDREVCURR@PERPRODCUSTCURR": This is the condition being checked. It compares the target revenue to the consensus demand revenue.
  • 1, 0: If the condition is true (target revenue is greater than consensus demand revenue), the formula returns 1. If false, it returns 0.

In essence, this formula sets the alert to 1 if the target revenue exceeds the consensus demand revenue, and 0 otherwise.

2. Activate Planning area.

Test Solution

1. Create planning view in microsoft excel

Select Attributes: Product ID

Select Key Figures: CONSENSUSDEMANDREVCURR, Target Revenue, Consensus demand quantity

In the Alerts Tab, select Revenue Alert, and in highlighted Key Figure, select Target Revenue and set Font color.

If the alert is triggered, Target Revenue will be highlighted with the Font Color.

In Period APR 2025, Target Revenue > Consensus demand Revenue, revenue alert is triggered, the target revenue in APR 2025 is turned to red.

entry.title: How to Speed-up SGEN Process【SAP/OS System Tuning】
entry.content:
The SGEN (SAP Load Generator) transaction is used to generate ABAP loads for various programs, function groups, and other components in SAP systems.

While it is a critical step in system maintenance and upgrades, it can be time-consuming. Below are strategies and considerations to optimize the SGEN process.

 

Key Strategies to Speed Up the SGEN Process

 

Increase the Number of Work Processes

If you only have one AP server and want to tune it easily, just increase the work processes from the RZ10 transaction. Since it depends on the case, I wrote also the information on the RZ12 for reference.

 

  • Parallel Processing: SGEN uses dialog work processes to compile ABAP programs. Increasing the number of parallel dialog work processes can significantly accelerate the process.

 

  • Use transaction RZ12 to configure the RFC group parallel_generators. This ensures that the maximum number of dialog processes is used for parallelization.

  • Adjust settings such as Max. Number of WPs Used (%) in RZ12 to allocate up to 90% of available work processes for SGEN if no other users are active during the process.

Optimize Server Resources

In many cases, performance tuning is possible simply by increasing the number of CPUs and work processes. You probably won't need more memory, IOPS or throughput.

In a cloud environment, it is easy to change the number of CPUs by changing the instance type. Choosing a CPU-specific instance will also improve cost performance 😄

 

  • CPU Configuration: The number of CPUs directly impacts SGEN performance. Systems with more CPUs can handle more parallel processes, reducing runtime.

  • Memory and Database Performance: Ensure sufficient memory and database performance as these are critical for handling the heavy load generated by SGEN. Tools like ST04 can help monitor database performance.

  • IOPS: High IOPS is essential for handling multiple small, random I/O operations efficiently. This is particularly important because SGEN generates ABAP loads, which involve frequent access to database tables like REPOSRC and REPOLOAD. Using storage solutions with high IOPS capabilities, such as NVMe SSDs, can significantly enhance performance.

  • Throughput: Throughput measures the volume of data transferred per second and is crucial for sequential read/write operations. For SGEN, which involves processing large amounts of data, high throughput ensures faster data transfer between storage and the application.

Balance Work Processes

  • Avoid overloading the server with too many work processes, as this can lead to inefficiencies or "thrashing." The optimal number of work processes depends on your server specifications.

  • Monitor CPU and memory utilization during SGEN execution to ensure resources are not overburdened.

Use Efficient Execution Modes

I shared information for the sake of best practices, but I think that in most cases you'd better to choose Generate All Objects of Selected Software Components


SGEN Execution Modes

  • Generate All Objects of Selected Software Components
    This mode generates ABAP loads for all objects within the selected software components. It is typically used after a system installation or upgrade

 

  • Resume Previous Generation Run
    This mode continues from where a previous SGEN run was interrupted, generating only the objects that were not completed earlier.

  • Regenerate Existing Loads
    • ALL: Regenerates all existing ABAP loads in the system.
    • INVALID: Regenerates only invalidated loads, which may occur after an upgrade or patch application.

  • Generate for Transport Requests
    Generates ABAP loads for objects included in specific transport requests.

  • Generate Business Server Pages (BSP) and Web Dynpro Applications
    Focuses on generating ABAP loads for BSP applications and Web Dynpro components.

Notes

Schedule During Off-Peak Hours

  • Run SGEN during times when system usage is minimal (e.g., weekends or nights) to maximize resource availability and minimize interference with other processes.

entry.title: Re: SAC - Advanced Formula Data Action - Calculate Prior...
entry.content:
@RameshBandi 

If you are worried about 12 month of data being queried, you can maintain an attribute in the version dimension for e.g START_DATE and maintain the CY jan as value so 202501. 

You can scope the date as MEMBERSET [d/Date]=[d/Version].[p/START_DATE] TO [d/Version].[p/START_DATE]

 

This will set the scope to 202501 only and only that records will be in scope for the 2 accounts. Now the below is only in reference to 202501 so no need for the IF statement to check for January.

DATA([d/Account]="Stat_PYAvgBal_AP") =   (RESULTLOOKUP([d/Account]="Stat_AP", [d/Date]=PREYEARLAST([d/Date])) +

    RESULTLOOKUP([d/Account]="Stat_AP", [d/Date]=PREVIOUS(13, "MONTH", [d/Date]))) / 2

FIRST/LAST/PREYEARLAST, None of them can be used in MEMBERSET. So only way I think using an attribute in Version dimension.

Hope this helps !!

Nikhil

entry.title: Re: Deployment of Organizational Structure from Developme...
entry.content:
The assumption is that development system doesn't need all the configuration as the development will be done on selected areas. Also, the configuration is expected to be done in the customizing tenant and so there is no need to transport from development tenant to customizing tenant. We do however understand, the concern with the initial setup and at this point as you said you have to activate the development tenant as well.

We do recommend activating the development tenant just like the customizing tenant so that when you develop something it will work in the customizing tenant.

Also currently there's no option available to copy the organizational structure from Customizing to Development, Each and every step like defining your scope, setting up primary finance settings and setting up organizational structure all this steps have to be performed manually for each project in Central Business Configuration.

we are working on this in the future.

Checkout below SAP learning page and the KBA:

3324929 - Not able to Import Collection into Customizing/Development Tenant (3SL) - SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Processing Transports in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

entry.title: Re: I created the SAP GUI AI Agent
entry.content:
Hi,

No, the model was not additionally trained in any way, nor was it given any ready-made scripts to execute. All it has is the ability to control individual actions in SAP-GUI, such as pressing buttons, filling in fields, or selecting options from a menu.

I think with your approach it would be possible to substitute ready-made scripts as tools. Then, automatic tests would probably be the most efficient.

On the other hand, a lot can also be achieved by simply giving the model detailed instructions on what to do. Note that in my videos, these instructions are quite general, yet the language model manages to use tools on its own. I usually don't even suggest which transaction to use, and it chooses on its own. However, ready instructions could be provided on how to behave in specific cases.

entry.title: Re: Convert DMS Folder ID to Name in Automation
entry.content:
Are you saying you have a path of a folder and you need to find the objectId so you can pass it back to the process to put in the approval form?

You would make a query like this:

https://{{endpoint}}/browser/{{repo}}/root/A?filter=cmis:objectId&cmisselector=object

This returns 4 properties of the folder at that path, including cmis:objectId.

I am assuming in your automation your doing some manipulation of documents in your DMS and you are putting documents in all kinds of folders.

entry.title: Re: Impact of ignoreErrors on Active Index in SolrIndexCo...
entry.content:
Hello! 

When you enable ignoreErrors, the goal is to prevent small problems from stopping the entire indexing process. The indexing job will continue running and log any errors it encounters along the way. However, whether the active index gets updated with data from a partially completed index depends on various factors, such as how the indexing job is set up.

Usually, a new index needs to be fully completed before it can replace or update the active index. If there are critical errors that stop the index from finishing, then the active index might not be updated. It's important to check the logs to see what errors occurred and understand how they might affect the completeness and accuracy of the new index.

Best regards,
Stephane
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Do you want to acquire foundational knowledge of SAP Business Data Cloud?

entry.title: Excel Upload Using RAP Application
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We are currently facing an issue with the file upload requirement using RAP model where the business needs to upload .XLS/.XLSX file from a custom RAP application. At the moment, we have successfully built the custom RAP app, which allows the upload of .CSV files, and we are able to process and alter the data as required. However, we are struggling to find a solution for uploading .XLS/.XLSX files.

Although we receive the data in RAWSTRING format using the Read Entity statement, we are unable to convert the RAWSTRING to our internal table. We are using the cl_abap_conv_codepage=>create_in()->convert method, but the system throws a dump at line: 'SYSTEM-CALL CONVERT ID 44' when attempting to call the convert method for .XLS/.XLSX files.

I have gone through multiple blogs shared on SAP Communities, but most solutions focus on uploading .CSV files. Could anyone provide guidance on this issue? We have several custom app development requirements, and this functionality will serve as a base for all of our file upload processes. Any priority support on this matter would be greatly appreciated!

For reference, I am pasting my uploadExcel method code below:

METHOD uploadexceldata.

READ ENTITIES OF zc_head_upd IN LOCAL MODE

ENTITY zc_head_upd

ALL FIELDS WITH

CORRESPONDING #( keys )

RESULT DATA(lt_inv).

 

** Get attachment value from the instance

DATA(lv_attachment) = lt_inv[ 1 ]-attachment.

 

** Data declarations

TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_excel_data,

mandt TYPE mandt,

svono TYPE aufnr,

startday TYPE char10,

zgroup TYPE char10,

area TYPE char10,

revtx TYPE revtx,

duration TYPE char2,

shift TYPE char2,

pitchid TYPE char50,

startpitch TYPE char4,

seq TYPE char2,

id TYPE char50,

lastchangedat TYPE abp_lastchange_tstmpl,

END OF ty_excel_data.

 

DATA: rows TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF string,

content TYPE string,

conv TYPE REF TO cl_abap_conv_codepage,

ls_excel_data TYPE ty_excel_data,

lt_excel_data TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_excel_data, "zps_dataupd_itm,

lt_excel_data_final TYPE TABLE OF zps_dataupd_itm,

lv_date TYPE char40,

lv_svono TYPE aufnr,

lv_timestamp TYPE timestampl.

 

DATA: lt_table TYPE TABLE OF standard.

 

GET TIME STAMP FIELD lv_timestamp.

lv_date = lv_timestamp.

 

content = cl_abap_conv_codepage=>create_in( )->convert( lv_attachment ).

 

** Split the string table to rows

SPLIT content AT cl_abap_char_utilities=>cr_lf INTO TABLE rows.

 

** Process the rows and append to the internal table

LOOP AT rows INTO DATA(ls_row) FROM 2.

SPLIT ls_row AT ',' INTO ls_excel_data-revtx

ls_excel_data-zgroup

ls_excel_data-svono

ls_excel_data-area

ls_excel_data-startday

ls_excel_data-duration

ls_excel_data-shift

ls_excel_data-pitchid

ls_excel_data-startpitch

ls_excel_data-seq

ls_excel_data-id.

 

ls_excel_data-lastchangedat = lv_date.

 

APPEND ls_excel_data TO lt_excel_data.

 

* LOOP AT lt_excel_data ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs>).

* <fs>-last_changed_at = lv_date.

* ENDLOOP.

 

 

CLEAR: ls_row, ls_excel_data.

 

LOOP AT lt_excel_data ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_excel_data>).

APPEND INITIAL LINE TO lt_excel_data_final ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_excel_final>).

MOVE-CORRESPONDING <fs_excel_data> TO <fs_excel_final>.

ENDLOOP.

 

MODIFY zps_dataupd_itm FROM TABLE lt_excel_data_final.

CLEAR: lt_excel_data, lt_excel_data_final.

ENDLOOP.

 

ENDMETHOD.

Best Regards

entry.title: Exploring the Potential of SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
Why SAP Business Data Cloud Matters

Data is the backbone of modern businesses. Companies that effectively analyze and leverage their data unlock valuable insights, much like striking gold—the deeper the analysis, the greater the rewards. SAP Business Data Cloud aims to break down data silos, allowing businesses to seamlessly connect data across SAP applications, third-party sources, and multiple business domains.

One of the most significant capabilities of SAP Business Data Cloud is its integration with Databricks, a market leader in data engineering and AI-powered analytics. This collaboration enables businesses to generate domain-specific insights by combining structured and unstructured data while reducing redundancy. Unlike traditional data solutions that require multiple copies of the same dataset, SAP Business Data Cloud enables zero-copy data access, making it particularly valuable for enterprises dealing with petabytes of data.

How Joule Enhances SAP Business Data Cloud

One of the standout features of SAP Business Data Cloud is its integration with Joule, SAP’s business AI assistant. What sets Joule apart from traditional Large Language Models (LLMs) is its deep business context and enterprise-grade data intelligence.

Joule’s architecture operates through the following key components:

 User Request Processing

  • When a user submits a request, Joule understands the prompt, analyzes relevant business scenarios, and applies data filtering via the Knowledge Catalog.

Knowledge Catalog

  • This repository contains both SAP data and customer-specific data, ensuring that responses are tailored to business needs.

Retrieval Augmented Generation for Enterprises (RAGe)

  • The output process leverages RAGe, an advanced AI methodology, ensuring responses are generated with reliable, context-aware business insights.

AI Hub and Vector Engine

  • The AI Hub integrates business context with SAP data, enhancing Joule’s ability to deliver accurate, business-centric queries from LLMs.

Driving Business Success with Unified Data

With SAP Business Data Cloud, Joule, and Databricks, organizations can unify enterprise data sources and unlock deeper insights tailored to various Lines of Business (LOBs). This intelligent data unification will empower companies to:
     

  • Eliminate redundant data storage
  • Generate multi-dimensional insights
  • Enhance AI-driven decision-making
  • Unlock revenue potential through data-driven strategies

By leveraging AI-powered analytics and zero-copy data integration, SAP is setting the stage for the next era of business intelligence, enabling companies to maximize data value while maintaining efficiency and security. 

Here is the link SAP Business Data Cloud preview

entry.title: Re: Enable Now experience for content authors after migra...
entry.content:
Hi Tracey,

There are definitely some differences between how you start Producer for On Premise versus how you start Producer for the Cloud solution and after opening Producer for the Cloud version you cannot switch between Workspaces.  However, you can open Producer for multiple Workspaces simultaneously, if you like.

Producer is very similar in both solutions, it's likely even the same executable code.  The really big differences between the two solutions are based on the Manager database.  If your version of Manager is installed on SQL Server, the Cloud version will have significantly more capability because it is installed on HANA.  If your On Premise version is already installed on HANA, the only major differences that you will notice, other than starting Producer and connecting to Workspaces, is if your version of On Premise is out of date.  Switching to Cloud obviously means that your system is permanently on the latest edition.

Regards
Shane

entry.title: SUBMIT rfitemgl problem
entry.content:
Hi guys 

I'm using submit to get data from FBL3n and put it in an internal table. The problem is: When I execute submit it's displaying the fbl3n selection screen and I don't want that, see my code:

FORM f_get_and_fill_data .

  FIELD-SYMBOLS: <fs_t_data> TYPE ANY TABLE,
                 <fs_s_data> TYPE any,
                 <fs_x_whs>  TYPE any,
                 <fs_saida>  TYPE ty_saida.

  DATA:  lf_ref              TYPE REF TO data,
         lt_texpr            TYPE rsds_texpr.

  " ********************************************************************
  " Chamada da Transação FBL3N
  " ********************************************************************

  " Ativa exportação de dados do ALV
  cl_salv_bs_runtime_info=>set(
    EXPORTING display  = abap_false
      metadata = abap_true
      data     = abap_true ).

*  SUBMIT zrsd242
*    WITH FREE SELECTIONS lt_texpr
*    WITH p_bukrs   EQ p_bukrs
*    WITH s_branch  IN s_branch
*    WITH s_docnum  IN s_docnum
*    WITH s_docdat  IN s_docdat
*    WITH s_oper    IN s_oper
*    WITH s_cat_nf  IN s_cat_nf
*    WITH p_j1b1n   EQ abap_false
*    WITH p_zt082   EQ abap_false
*    WITH p_dois    EQ abap_true
*    WITH r_detail  EQ abap_true
*    WITH r_sintet  EQ abap_false
*  USING SELECTION-SCREEN 1000 AND
*        RETURN EXPORTING LIST TO MEMORY.

SUBMIT rfitemgl
WITH FREE SELECTIONS lt_texpr
  WITH p_SAKNR IN p_SAKNR
  WITH p_wlbuk IN p_wlbuk
  WITH p_budat IN p_budat
  WITH x_opsel  EQ abap_false
  WITH x_clsel  EQ abap_false
  WITH x_aisel  EQ abap_true
  WITH x_norm   EQ abap_true
*  WITH pa_vari  EQ '/OES_EXT'
  USING SELECTION-SCREEN 1000 AND
        RETURN EXPORTING LIST TO MEMORY.

  " ********************************************************************
  " Recupera tabela ALV
  " ********************************************************************
  TRY.
      cl_salv_bs_runtime_info=>get_data_ref( IMPORTING r_data = lf_ref ).
      ASSIGN lf_ref->* TO <fs_t_data>.
    CATCH cx_salv_bs_sc_runtime_info.
      MESSAGE 'Erro ao recuperar dados do ALV - zt0388' TYPE 'E'.
  ENDTRY.

  cl_salv_bs_runtime_info=>clear_all( ).

  IF <fs_t_data>[] IS ASSIGNED.
    IF NOT <fs_t_data> IS INITIAL.
      LOOP AT <fs_t_data> ASSIGNING <fs_s_data>.
        MOVE-CORRESPONDING <fs_s_data> TO gs_saida.
        gs_saida-datum = sy-datum.
        gs_saida-uzeit = sy-uzeit.
        APPEND gs_saida TO gt_saida.
      ENDLOOP.
    ELSE.
      MESSAGE text-e01 TYPE 'S' DISPLAY LIKE 'E'.
      LEAVE LIST-PROCESSING.
    ENDIF.
  ELSE.
    MESSAGE text-e01 TYPE 'S' DISPLAY LIKE 'E'.
    LEAVE LIST-PROCESSING.
  ENDIF.

entry.title: Re: SAP ABAP Platform 1909, Developer Edition: See New Blog Post
entry.content:
The passwords are on the github page: https://github.com/SAP-docs/abap-platform-trial-image

The SYSTEM password for SYSTEMDB and HDB are 1) the same and 2) in the link above.

You will need to create the container using -p 30215:30215 as part of the docker run command line if you want to expose the tenant SQL port outside the container.

Use:

docker exec -it a4h bash

#sudo -iu hdbadm

$ hdbsql -n vhcala4hci:30213 -d SYSTEMDB -u SYSTEM

<password>

to get access to the SYSTEMDB and

$ hdbsql -n vhcala4hci:30215 -d HDB -u SYSTEM

<password>

to get access to the tenant database.

SELECT * FROM M_DATABASE;

will show you what database you're in

(Probably loads of typos above)

entry.title: Re: Converting HTML to PDF in ABAP
entry.content:
Hi Purayil,

ABAP doesn’t offer a built-in way to convert arbitrary HTML to PDF—it mainly supports conversion for SAP-specific formats like Smart Forms, SAPscript, or ALV lists. For your case, where you're working with dynamic HTML, you'll need to use an external solution.

One option is to use a dedicated HTML-to-PDF conversion API, such as PDFBolt. With such an API, you can send your HTML content via an HTTP request and receive a PDF in response, which you can then attach to your email using ABAP’s standard emailing functions (e.g., BCS).

Alternatively, if your environment allows, you might set up a command-line tool on your server that performs the conversion, and then call that tool from ABAP.

Hope this helps!

entry.title: Re: NF-e Nota Técnica 2023.004 v1.10: novos campos adicionados à nota fiscal
entry.content:
Olá pessoal,

Alguém que utilize o Mastersaf DFE SaaS e que tenha aplicado as notas da NT 2023.004 v1.20, teve algum problema com relação ao novo campo CPF?

Mais precisamente quando a Forma de Importação é '1' (Importação por conta própria) e, consequentemente, os campos CNPJ e CPF não devem ser preenchidos. Nesse caso o campo CPF, que é numérico, fica '00000000000' na estrutura passada para a RFC. Até aí, tudo bem.

No entanto, o XML está sendo gerado com a tag do CPF preenchida com "zeros" e com isso o DFE retorna o erro "conteúdo inválido.

A Thomson, desenvolvedora do Mastersaf DFE, insiste que não há problemas nas rotinas deles.

Qualquer ajuda é bem vinda. Agradeço desde já.

entry.title: Re: The fastest way to a deployable CAP app on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
@daniel_schlachter 

Thank you for sharing this. I have tried `cds add workzone` in my TypeScript-enabled application generated by Fiori tools and found that the following parts of the code need to be updated.

manifest.json: The leading "/" of the uri must be removed.

 

    "dataSources": {
      "mainService": {
        "uri": "odata/v4/catalog/",

 

ui5-deploy.yaml: ui5-tooling-transpile-task needed to be added to generate .js files.

 

  customTasks:
    - name: ui5-tooling-transpile-task
      afterTask: replaceVersion
      configuration:
        debug: true
        transformModulesToUI5:
          overridesToOverride: true
    - name: ui5-task-zipper
      afterTask: generateVersionInfo
      configuration:
        archiveName: books-ui
        additionalFiles:
          - xs-app.json

entry.title: Re: Accessing MIGO Through Fiori Launchpad on Mobile Device
entry.content:
We discussed this topic within the Personas team, and if we understand the situation correctly, the problem is that when using a mobile device, the classic app MIGO is disabled in Public Cloud. Therefore, the tile accessing it is disabled in FLP. However, it is possible to access this app when using a desktop browser.

If our understanding is correct, then there could be a possibility to create a custom tile based on this blog post. A tile pointing to a URL utilizing the webgui or mobgui service should do the trick.

Of course, it is necessary to configure Personas access via an assignment category and setting the default flavor for users to the intended MIGO parent flavor. The adaptive flavor selection will then take care of using the appropriate flavor for the device.

 When I debug attachEvent, only events messageChange and sessionTimeout are supported. UI5 version is 1.102.2.

entry.title: Re: Stock Transfer between two EWM Managed plants assigne...
entry.content:
Ideally 2 plants assigned to one warehouse and doing a SLOC to SLOC via Bin to Bin should not be a scenario.

You can achieve it if you do not assign any availability group to Storage Types that will allow you to do bin to bin and then you will have to do a posting change of Ownership/PartyEntitled. That will post the stock to other SLOC.

I'm not sure if there is something I missed while creating the custom field or it is a SAP bug. Can someone help?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Which SAP Sales Cloud Capabilities make sense for your business?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community! 


When it comes to sales, every business has its own way of working. But one thing is true for everyone: the right tools can make a huge difference in how smoothly things run. That’s why it’s important to take a step back and ask—are we using the right capabilities to support our sales team?

With SAP Sales Cloud, there are many capabilities designed to help sales teams work smarter, not harder. The challenge isn’t just having these tools but knowing which ones will actually make an impact. For example, if keeping track of customer interactions feels messy, Account and Contact Management can help centralize information so teams can collaborate better. If you want to improve forecasting and visibility into your pipeline, Opportunity and Pipeline Management can provide a clearer picture of sales progress. Or maybe you need to improve pricing accuracy—Price Management can help ensure consistency across deals. Need better customer engagement? Sales Lead Management helps track and nurture leads to increase conversion rates. If your team spends too much time on manual processes, Order Capture can simplify sales by automating order entry and connecting with backend systems.

To help businesses identify the capabilities that fit their needs, we have created a conversation of value called Increase Sales Force Efficiency, this is a great starting point to explore the different capabilities in SAP Sales Cloud and identify which one makes sense for your business. Additionally, we’ve developed a more detailed e-learning course, Value Package to Increase Your Sales Force Efficiency, which provides a deeper look into these capabilities, along with extra guidance and additional resources. 

Both assets are available in SAP Learning Hub. To access them, you'll need to complete a one-time registration. You can find all the details and sign up here: SAP Learning Hub, edition for SAP Enterprise Support.

Investing time in the right sales capabilities can make a big difference in how your team works and how your business grows. Whether you're looking to improve efficiency, streamline processes, or give your sales team better tools, SAP Sales Cloud has a range of features to help you.

Easy steps, amazing CX!

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

About the author:

Edmar Rodríguez is a Topic Expert for the SAP Customer Experience Value Map. He helps customers make the most of their SAP Enterprise Support maintenance contract by guiding them through the available tools and services at their disposal. 

Have questions or want to keep the conversation going? We’d love to hear from you! Feel free to reach out to the SAP Customer Experience Value Map team at SAPESValueMaps@sap.com. And if you haven’t yet, sign up for the SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps to explore more resources, best practices, and expert guidance.

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

entry.title: Re: Condition Contract - Distribution process
entry.content:
Hi @Miche1984, 

I’m curious about the need to post the difference in accrual separately—is there a specific reason for this approach? Would it not be more effective to apply the logic of reversing open accruals only up to the net settlement amount?

It is now possible (though I don’t recall the exact version in which this was introduced) to restrict the system from reversing more accruals than the net settlement amount in a manual settlement process.

To apply this logic, you need to:

  1. Maintain Routine 91 in the manual pricing procedure for the REA5 condition type (or its equivalent).
  2. Activate the relevant setting in the Process Variant configuration (see below).



Another approach would be to add that logic to reverse open accrual up to net amount in a custom routine in copy of 91 only for manual settlement, if the above solution is not possible for existing live contract. 

Regards, 
Gauthier VICTOR

entry.title: Modification of accounting accounts for their allocation from the maintenance order
entry.content:
Hello Experts, I have a question regarding the implementation of the PM module or has the following case arisen in any company or do you think it is possible?

The scenario is about being able to modify the accounting account of the material from the maintenance order,

That is, if a material example: X2569 - points to the accounting account that has the name "Masonry" and is used in maintenance order #1 of a "civil engineering" service, it is correct. 
But if in order #2 the same material X2569 is required but this service is for "Plumbing" that in the maintenance order it can be modified and the corresponding accounting account can be placed which will affect in this case the accounting account called "plumbing".

Is this possible if I have a wide catalog of accounts depending on the maintenance specialty?

Thank you very much in advance for instructing me.
Best regards
Ana

entry.title: Re: SAP DBTech JDBC: [403]: internal error: Cannot get re...
entry.content:
Unfortunately, there is no easy answer for cases of an initial connection timeout. My presumption would be that something is tying up the connection attempt unrelated to the target database, i.e.:

  • Firewall
  • Proxy/Reverse Proxy Server
  • Load balancer

That said, some reasons why HANA may not respond in time to a new connection request (resulting in such timeouts) include:

  • Low available compute resources (near 100% CPU utilization)
  • System tied up in memory resource management (OOM, resource container shrinks, etc.)
  • Failover to a replication site

Considering the context of this post (attempting to register a remote HANA database for smart data access) my feeling would be that this is likely related to network infrastructure.

If you would like to take the SAP software out of the equation to validate that there is an external problem, I would suggest testing with another ODBC client (e.g. isql) using the same HANA ODBC driver. This is a nice sanity check to ensure that all ODBC clients are similarly affected.

entry.title: Schema in Document Information Extraction for Create Sales Order - Automatic Extraction (4X9)
entry.content:
I have set up scope item 4X9 Create Sales Orders - Automatic Extraction in my S/4HANA 2023 Private Cloud environment. When uploading the purchase order PDF, there are corrections I would like to make that I have actioned in the Document Information Extraction application on BTP. I have corrected the fields, associated the document with the template, and assigned the template to schema SAP_purchaseOrder_schema.

When I upload the PDF in the Document Information Extraction UI, I can select this schema and the template is applied correctly. 

However, when using the UI in S/4HANA, in this case the "Create Sales Order - Automatic Extraction" fiori app, the document shows up in Document Information Extraction UI but it shows the message

"Schema associated with this document has been deleted."

Is this expected behaviour? Is it possible to associate this app "Create Sales Order - Automatic Extraction" (Scope item 4X9) with a particular schema for Purchase Orders? Or perhaps change the association in Document Information Extraction to use the schema SAP_purchaseOrder_schema when the inbound document is a Purchase Order, which is correctly identified in the header.

The plan I have selected in BTP for Document Information Extraction is the basic plan, not the premium plan.

Any guidance is much appreciated.

entry.title: Re: SAP Fiori launchpad: Hiding empty spaces and pages
entry.content:
Hi Sergio.,

At this stage we are expecting that it will be included with SAP S/4HANA release 2025. That is yet to be officially confirmed so please keep watch for that. 

Worth knowing that SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 and higher already has the merging features for spaces and pages which reduce the need for hiding empty pages.
